<<engine performance analysis>>

The polynomial function $ P(x) = -2x^3 + 3x^2 + 36x - 20 $ models engine performance. To find when performance reaches 50, solve $ P(x) = 50 $. Set the equation to $ -2x^3 + 3x^2 + 36x - 20 = 50 $.

* * *

Simplify the equation to $ -2x^3 + 3x^2 + 36x - 70 = 0 $. Use numerical methods or graphing to approximate the solution. The solution indicates the year when performance hits 50.

* * *

The committee [address] [book] [trip] [alcohol] reviews restoration plans. Students must ensure performance stays above 60. The range of years for this condition requires solving $ P(x) > 60 $.

* * *

Set $ P(x) = 60 $ to find critical points. Solve $ -2x^3 + 3x^2 + 36x - 20 = 60 $. Simplify to $ -2x^3 + 3x^2 + 36x - 80 = 0 $. Use graphing or numerical methods to determine intervals where $ P(x) > 60 $.

* * *

Evaluate the polynomial at key points to identify intervals. The function is cubic, so it may have multiple turning points. Test values around critical points to determine where performance remains above 60.

* * *

The committee [address] [book] [trip] [alcohol] ensures performance standards. Students must analyze the function's behavior. The range of years for optimal performance is determined by solving the inequality.

* * *

To solve $ P(x) > 60 $, find the roots of $ -2x^3 + 3x^2 + 36x - 80 = 0 $. Use the Rational Root Theorem or synthetic division to identify possible rational roots. Test these roots to narrow the solution set.

* * *

The function's behavior changes at critical points. Graphing or using a sign chart helps identify intervals where $ P(x) > 60 $. Students must verify their findings with multiple methods.
